preview_text:
The model favors Japan at 69%, a substantial margin over Sweden's 8% win probability. This asymmetry reflects a clear gap in the statistical forecast, with Japan positioned as the heavy favorite on neutral ground.

The spread reveals a moderately competitive match in expected shape. Japan's win probability nearly three times that of Sweden's draw and loss combined suggests the model anticipates a fairly one-sided contest, though the 24% draw probability indicates the outcome is not predetermined. Matches of this structure typically see one team dominate possession and chances, though variance always permits surprises.

The model and sportsbook consensus diverge meaningfully. Bookmakers price Japan at 50%, roughly 19 percentage points below the model's 69%. Polymarket prices align closer to sportsbooks, at 52%. This gap suggests either that market participants assess the teams as nearer parity than the statistical model, or that the model's Elo inputs overstate Japan's edge. The dashboard does not adjudicate between these sources—only that they diverge substantially.
